---
title: "A mizer model with von Bertalanffy growth"
author: "Gustav Delius"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{A mizer model with von Bertalanffy growth}
  %\VignetteEngine{quarto::html}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

## Introduction

The function `newVonBertalanffyParams()` builds a
[mizer](https://sizespectrum.org/mizer/) model whose individuals, in the
steady state of the model, grow along a prescribed von Bertalanffy growth
curve. This is less immediate than it sounds. In mizer the growth of an
individual is not a parameter but an **emergent** quantity: it is what is left
of the assimilated food intake after paying for metabolism, and of that surplus
only the part not diverted into reproduction contributes to somatic growth. To
make this emergent growth reproduce a given von Bertalanffy curve we therefore
have to choose the feeding, metabolic and reproductive terms with some care.

This vignette derives the construction from the ground up. We start from the von
Bertalanffy curve itself, translate it into the energy budget that mizer uses,
add reproduction, and finally deal with the awkward case of a negative
von Bertalanffy age offset $t_0$, where a naive implementation produces a
discontinuity in the growth rate at maturity and where we show how to avoid it.

The von Bertalanffy parameters are supplied in the `w_inf`, `k_vb` and `t0`
columns of the species parameter data frame, together with the weight–length
exponent `b`, the maturity weight `w_mat` and the maximum weight `w_max`.

## Von Bertalanffy growth in length and weight

The von Bertalanffy model describes growth in length $l$ by

$$
\frac{dl}{dt} = k_{vb}\,(L_\infty - l),
\qquad
l(t) = L_\infty\left(1 - e^{-k_{vb}(t - t_0)}\right),
$$ {#eq-vb-length}

where $L_\infty$ is the asymptotic length, $k_{vb}$ the growth rate constant and
$t_0$ the (hypothetical) age at which length is zero.

Mizer works in terms of weight $w$, related to length by the allometric law
$w = a\,l^{b}$, so that the asymptotic weight is $w_\infty = a\,L_\infty^{b}$. It
is convenient to introduce the variable

$$
u = w^{1/b} = a^{1/b}\, l ,
\qquad
u_\infty = w_\infty^{1/b} = a^{1/b}\, L_\infty ,
$$

which is simply length up to the constant factor $a^{1/b}$. Because $u$ is
proportional to $l$, it satisfies the same linear equation,

$$
\frac{du}{dt} = k_{vb}\,(u_\infty - u),
\qquad
u(t) = u_\infty\left(1 - e^{-k_{vb}(t - t_0)}\right).
$$ {#eq-vb-u}

Transforming back to weight with $w = u^{b}$ and
$\frac{dw}{dt} = b\,u^{b-1}\frac{du}{dt}$, and using $u^{b-1} = w^{\,1-1/b}$,
gives the von Bertalanffy growth rate in weight,

$$
\frac{dw}{dt} = g_{vb}(w) = A\,w^{n} - B\,w
$$
where
$$
n = 1 - \frac1b,\quad
A = b\,k_{vb}\,w_\infty^{1/b},\quad
B = b\,k_{vb}.
$$ {#eq-vb-weight}

This is the growth rate returned by `vonBertalanffyGrowth()`. It is positive for
$w < w_\infty$ and vanishes at $w = w_\infty$, where $A\,w_\infty^{n} = B\,w_\infty$.
The exponent relation $n = 1 - 1/b$ is what ties the mizer growth exponent to the
weight–length exponent $b$.

## The mizer energy budget

In mizer an individual of weight $w$ encounters food at a rate $E(w)$ and can
ingest at most $h\,w^{n}$ (the maximum intake). The fraction of the maximum that
is actually realised is the *feeding level*
$f = E / (E + h\,w^{n})$, and the assimilated energy income is
$\alpha\,E\,(1 - f)$, where $\alpha$ is the assimilation efficiency. After
subtracting the metabolic loss rate $\mathrm{metab}(w)$, the energy available for
growth and reproduction is

$$
E_r(w) = \alpha\,E(w)\,\big(1 - f(w)\big) - \mathrm{metab}(w).
$$

The construction removes every complication that would make $E_r$ deviate from a
pure power law:

- **Satiation is switched off** by setting $h = \infty$, so $f = 0$ and the
  income is simply $\alpha\,E(w)$.
- **Predation and resource interactions are switched off**, so all of $E(w)$ is
  an externally prescribed encounter rate rather than something that depends on
  the evolving spectrum.
- **The metabolic loss is linear**, $\mathrm{metab}(w) = B\,w$, obtained by
  setting the standard-metabolism coefficient to zero and the activity
  coefficient to $k = b\,k_{vb} = B$.

With these choices we only have to fix the encounter rate so that the income is
the power law $\alpha\,E(w) = A\,w^{n}$, i.e.

$$
E(w) = \frac{A}{\alpha}\,w^{n}.
$$

The energy available for growth and reproduction is then exactly the von
Bertalanffy rate,

$$
E_r(w) = \alpha\,E(w) - \mathrm{metab}(w) = A\,w^{n} - B\,w = g_{vb}(w).
$$ {#eq-Er}

Before we account for reproduction, an individual therefore grows at precisely
the von Bertalanffy rate.

## External mortality

For completeness we note that the model also imposes a power-law external
mortality $\mu(w) = \mu_0\, w^{\,n-1}$, with the coefficient $\mu_0$ chosen so
that the steady-state juvenile biomass density $w\,N(w)$ has a small negative
slope (here $-0.2$). This gives a realistic steady-state size spectrum but does
not influence the individual growth curve, which is our concern here, so we do
not dwell on it.

## Reproduction

Mizer does not put all of the surplus energy $E_r$ into somatic growth. A
size-dependent fraction $\psi(w)$, the *maturity ogive*, is diverted into
reproduction, so that the **somatic** growth rate is

$$
g(w) = \big(1 - \psi(w)\big)\,E_r(w),
$$ {#eq-somatic}

and the remaining $\psi(w)\,E_r(w)$ funds egg production. The ogive rises
smoothly from $0$ below the maturity weight $w_{mat}$ towards $1$ at the
reproduction cut-off weight $w_{\text{repro,max}}$. Above $w_{mat}$ its switching
factor has essentially saturated and

$$
\psi(w) \;\approx\; \left(\frac{w}{w_{\text{repro,max}}}\right)^{1-n}
\qquad (w \gtrsim w_{mat}).
$$ {#eq-psi}

If we did nothing, @eq-somatic would make the somatic growth
$(1-\psi)\,g_{vb}$, which falls below the von Bertalanffy curve as soon as
$\psi > 0$. To keep the *somatic* growth on the von Bertalanffy curve we need the
surplus energy to satisfy $(1-\psi)\,E_r = g_{vb}$, that is

$$
E_r(w) = \frac{g_{vb}(w)}{1 - \psi(w)}.
$$

Since the unmodified budget @eq-Er already delivers $E_r = g_{vb}$, we achieve
this by **reducing the metabolic loss** by

$$
\Delta_{\text{repro}}(w)
= \frac{g_{vb}(w)}{1-\psi(w)} - g_{vb}(w)
= \frac{\psi(w)}{1-\psi(w)}\,g_{vb}(w).
$$ {#eq-repro}

The reduced energy spent on metabolism is exactly the energy that now flows into
reproduction, so the total budget still balances.

It is worth checking that this reduction never exceeds the metabolic loss itself,
which would make the metabolic rate negative. Setting
$w_{\text{repro,max}} = w_\infty$, so that $\psi = (w/w_\infty)^{1-n} = u/u_\infty$
in the saturated regime, and writing $g_{vb}$ in the variable $u$ as
$g_{vb} = b\,k_{vb}\,u^{\,b-1}(u_\infty - u)$, we find

$$
\begin{split}
\Delta_{\text{repro}}
&= \frac{u/u_\infty}{1 - u/u_\infty}\; b\,k_{vb}\,u^{\,b-1}(u_\infty - u)\\
&= \frac{u}{u_\infty - u}\; b\,k_{vb}\,u^{\,b-1}(u_\infty - u)\\
&= b\,k_{vb}\,u^{b}
= B\,w
= \mathrm{metab}(w).
\end{split}
$$ {#eq-repro-identity}

This identity holds throughout the mature range where the ogive has saturated,
$\psi = (w/w_\infty)^{1-n}$: there the reproduction investment equals the entire
metabolic loss, so the reduced metabolic rate is zero and the power-law income
$A\,w^{n}$ is shared between somatic growth $g_{vb}$ and reproduction. In the
maturation transition just above $w_{mat}$ the ogive has not yet saturated,
$\psi$ is smaller, and the reduction is strictly less than $B\,w$. Either way it
never exceeds $B\,w$, so the metabolic rate stays non-negative (and somatic
growth stops only at $w_\infty$, where $g_{vb} = 0$).

## A negative age offset $t_0$

Everything so far assumed that a fish starting from the egg weight $w_{min}$ at
age $0$ can follow the von Bertalanffy curve. This breaks down when the fitted
$t_0$ is **negative**. From @eq-vb-length the von Bertalanffy curve then places
the fish at a *positive* size already at age zero,

$$
w(0) = w_\infty\left(1 - e^{k_{vb} t_0}\right)^{b} > w_{min},
$$

because $e^{k_{vb} t_0} < 1$ when $t_0 < 0$. A biological birth at a negative age
makes no sense, and mizer must start every fish at the egg weight $w_{min}$,
which is far below $w(0)$. The model simply cannot follow the von Bertalanffy
curve over the whole juvenile range.

What we *can* ask for is that the fish reaches the maturity weight $w_{mat}$ at
the age the von Bertalanffy curve predicts, and that it grows along the von
Bertalanffy curve from maturity onwards. Inverting @eq-vb-u, the von Bertalanffy
age at maturity is

$$
t_{mat}
= t_0 - \frac{1}{k_{vb}}\log\!\left(1 - \frac{u_{mat}}{u_\infty}\right)
= t_0 - \frac{1}{k_{vb}}\log\!\left(1 - \left(\frac{w_{mat}}{w_\infty}\right)^{1/b}\right),
$$ {#eq-tmat}

with $u_{mat} = w_{mat}^{1/b}$. A negative $t_0$ makes $t_{mat}$ smaller: the fish
must reach maturity *sooner* than the plain von Bertalanffy rate would allow from
$w_{min}$. We therefore have to speed up growth below maturity.

## The problem with a constant speed-up

The obvious way to speed up juvenile growth is to lower the metabolic loss below
maturity by a constant factor $c \in (0,1]$, replacing $\mathrm{metab} = B\,w$
with $c\,B\,w$ for $w < w_{mat}$. The growth rate below maturity becomes
$A\,w^{n} - c\,B\,w$, which in the variable $u$ is again a von Bertalanffy form,

$$
\frac{du}{dt} = c\,k_{vb}\left(\frac{u_\infty}{c} - u\right),
$$

with rate $c\,k_{vb}$ and a raised asymptote $u_\infty/c$. Its age from $w_{min}$
to $w_{mat}$ has the closed form

$$
\mathrm{age}(c) = \frac{1}{c\,k_{vb}}
\log\!\frac{u_\infty - c\,u_{min}}{u_\infty - c\,u_{mat}},
$$

which decreases as $c$ decreases, so one can try to solve $\mathrm{age}(c) = t_{mat}$
for $c$. The fastest growth this can produce is at $c = 0$ (no metabolic loss at
all); if even that is too slow, one has to inject extra energy by raising the
intake instead.

The trouble is that this speed-up is applied at **full strength right up to**
$w_{mat}$. At maturity the growth rate jumps from $A\,w_{mat}^{n} - c\,B\,w_{mat}$
just below to the von Bertalanffy value $A\,w_{mat}^{n} - B\,w_{mat}$ just above —
a downward step of size $(1-c)\,B\,w_{mat}$. There is a second, subtler jump as
well: if the reproduction compensation of @eq-repro is simply switched off below
$w_{mat}$, then, because the ogive $\psi$ is already positive just below
maturity, the somatic growth there carries an uncompensated factor $(1-\psi)$ and
jumps by that factor at $w_{mat}$. Either way the growth rate is discontinuous at
maturity, which shows up as a kink in the growth curve.

```{r}
#| label: fig-discontinuity
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 7
#| fig-height: 4.2
#| fig-cap: "Growth rate near the maturity weight for a species with a negative
#|   $t_0$ (illustrative parameters). The constant-factor speed-up (dashed)
#|   reaches maturity at the von Bertalanffy age but drops discontinuously onto
#|   the von Bertalanffy curve at $w_{mat}$. The tapering speed-up (solid) hits
#|   the same maturity age while joining the von Bertalanffy curve continuously.
#|   Both coincide with the von Bertalanffy rate (grey) above maturity."

# Illustrative parameters (a mildly negative t0, so the constant-factor
# speed-up is feasible and its discontinuity can be shown cleanly).
b <- 3; k <- 0.38; w_inf <- 430; t0 <- -0.3; w_mat <- 69; w_min <- 1e-3
n <- 1 - 1 / b
A <- b * k * w_inf^(1 / b); B <- b * k
uf <- function(w) w^(1 / b)
u_inf <- uf(w_inf); u_mat <- uf(w_mat); u_min <- uf(w_min)

g_vb <- function(w) A * w^n - B * w
t_mat <- t0 - log(1 - u_mat / u_inf) / k

# constant-factor speed-up: solve for c
age_c <- function(c) log((u_inf - c * u_min) / (u_inf - c * u_mat)) / (c * k)
c_star <- uniroot(function(c) age_c(c) - t_mat, c(1e-8, 1))$root
g_const <- function(w) ifelse(w < w_mat, A * w^n - c_star * B * w, g_vb(w))

# tapering speed-up: Delta = s * w^n * (1 - (w / w_mat)^(1 - n)), solve for s.
# g_vb + Delta is again a von Bertalanffy form, so the age has a closed form.
Delta <- function(w, s) ifelse(w < w_mat, s * w^n * (1 - (w / w_mat)^(1 - n)), 0)
age_s <- function(s) {
    A_t <- A + s; B_t <- B + s / u_mat; k_t <- B_t / b   # modified vB coefficients
    log((A_t / B_t - u_min) / (A_t / B_t - u_mat)) / k_t
}
s_star <- uniroot(function(s) age_s(s) - t_mat, c(0, 1e5))$root
g_taper <- function(w) g_vb(w) + Delta(w, s_star)

w <- seq(w_min, 1.6 * w_mat, length.out = 600)
plot(w, g_vb(w), type = "l", col = "grey70", lwd = 6,
     xlab = "weight w", ylab = "growth rate dw/dt",
     ylim = c(0, max(g_const(w))))
lines(w, g_const(w), col = "firebrick", lwd = 2, lty = 2)
lines(w, g_taper(w), col = "steelblue", lwd = 2)
abline(v = w_mat, col = "grey40", lty = 3)
text(w_mat, 0, expression(w[mat]), pos = 4, col = "grey40")
legend("topleft", bty = "n",
       col = c("grey70", "firebrick", "steelblue"),
       lwd = c(6, 2, 2), lty = c(1, 2, 1),
       legend = c("von Bertalanffy", "constant speed-up (discontinuous)",
                  "tapering speed-up (continuous)"))
```

## A tapering speed-up

The fix is to make the speed-up **vanish at maturity** so that the growth rate
joins the von Bertalanffy curve continuously. Instead of a constant reduction we
add a term $\Delta(w)$ to the growth rate that tapers to zero at $w_{mat}$:

$$
\Delta(w) =
\begin{cases}
s\,w^{n}\left(1 - \left(\dfrac{w}{w_{mat}}\right)^{1-n}\right), & w < w_{mat},\\[2ex]
0, & w \ge w_{mat},
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-delta}

with a single amplitude $s \ge 0$. The factor $1 - (w/w_{mat})^{1-n}$ vanishes at
$w_{mat}$ and is positive below (since $1 - n = 1/b > 0$), so $\Delta$ is
non-negative and vanishes at maturity. The target somatic growth

$$
g_{\text{target}}(w) = g_{vb}(w) + \Delta(w)
$$ {#eq-gtarget}

is therefore continuous at maturity, equal to $g_{vb}$ above it and faster below.

The reason for this particular shape is that $\Delta$ is again of von Bertalanffy
form. Expanding,

$$
\Delta(w) = s\,w^{n} - \frac{s}{u_{mat}}\,w
= A_\Delta\,w^{n} + B_\Delta\,w,
\qquad
A_\Delta = s,\quad B_\Delta = -\frac{s}{u_{mat}},
$$

using $w_{mat}^{-(1-n)} = w_{mat}^{\,n-1} = 1/u_{mat}$. Hence the below-maturity
growth

$$
g_{\text{target}}(w) = (A + s)\,w^{n} - \Big(B + \tfrac{s}{u_{mat}}\Big)\,w
= \tilde A\,w^{n} - \tilde B\,w
$$

is itself a von Bertalanffy rate, with a faster growth constant
$\tilde k = \tilde B / b = k_{vb} + s/(b\,u_{mat})$ and a lower asymptote
$\tilde u_\infty = \tilde A/\tilde B \in (u_{mat}, u_\infty)$. Below maturity the
fish thus grows along a von Bertalanffy curve that has been steepened just enough
to reach $w_{mat}$ on time, and which crosses the original curve exactly at
$w_{mat}$.

Because the below-maturity growth is a von Bertalanffy rate, the age to reach
maturity has the same closed form as in @eq-vb-u, and the amplitude $s$ is fixed
by requiring maturity at the von Bertalanffy age @eq-tmat:

$$
\mathrm{age}(s) =
\frac{1}{\tilde k}\,
\log\frac{\tilde u_\infty - u_{min}}{\tilde u_\infty - u_{mat}} = t_{mat}.
$$ {#eq-age-s}

As $s$ increases from $0$, $\tilde k$ grows and $\tilde u_\infty$ falls towards
$u_{mat}$, so $\mathrm{age}(s)$ decreases monotonically from the plain von
Bertalanffy transit time at $s = 0$ towards $0$ as $s \to \infty$. Any target age
$t_{mat} > 0$ is therefore reachable — unlike the constant-factor scheme, there is
no lower bound on the attainable maturity age. Equation @eq-age-s is a single
closed-form expression, solved for $s$ numerically.

### Realising it in the energy budget

It remains to realise $g_{\text{target}}$ within the mizer budget while keeping
the reproduction compensation of @eq-repro. Because the below-maturity target is
itself a von Bertalanffy rate $\tilde A\,w^{n} - \tilde B\,w$, we realise it in
exactly the way the main curve was built in @eq-Er and @eq-repro: the $w^{n}$
term is carried by the (allometric) encounter income and the $w$ term by the
(linear) metabolic loss, with the reproduction investment again drawn from the
metabolic loss. Writing

$$
\tilde A(w) = A + s\,\mathbb{1}[w < w_{mat}],
\qquad
\tilde B(w) = B + \frac{s}{u_{mat}}\,\mathbb{1}[w < w_{mat}],
$$

so that $\tilde A\,w^{n} - \tilde B\,w = g_{\text{target}}(w)$, we set

$$
E(w) = \frac{\tilde A(w)\,w^{n}}{\alpha},
\qquad
\mathrm{metab}(w) = \tilde B(w)\,w \;-\; \frac{\psi(w)}{1-\psi(w)}\,g_{\text{target}}(w).
$$ {#eq-split}

The subtracted term is the reproduction investment, generalising @eq-repro from
$g_{vb}$ to the target growth $g_{\text{target}}$; it stays below $\tilde B\,w$
(as in @eq-repro-identity), so the metabolic loss remains non-negative. One
checks directly that $\alpha E - \mathrm{metab} = g_{\text{target}}/(1-\psi)$, and
hence, from @eq-somatic, the somatic growth is exactly
$(1-\psi)\,g_{\text{target}}/(1-\psi) = g_{\text{target}}$, as intended.

This is the decomposition we want: the speed-up raises the **encounter income**
through its $w^{n}$ term ($A \to A + s$) and the **metabolic loss** through its
$w$ term ($B \to B + s/u_{mat}$), rather than being absorbed into a reduction of
the metabolic loss. Consumption therefore stays allometric ($\propto w^{n}$) and
respiration linear ($\propto w$) throughout. Below maturity, where $\psi \approx
0$, this is simply $\alpha E = (A+s)\,w^{n}$ and $\mathrm{metab} = (B+s/u_{mat})\,w$.

The construction avoids both discontinuities of the previous section. The
constant-factor jump is gone because $\Delta$ tapers to zero at $w_{mat}$; the
reproduction jump is gone because the reproduction investment
$\tfrac{\psi}{1-\psi}g_{\text{target}}$ is applied continuously across maturity
rather than switched off below it. The growth rate is therefore continuous. The
encounter and metabolic rates *individually* step by $s\,w_{mat}^{n}$ at $w_{mat}$
— the coefficients $\tilde A, \tilde B$ switch there — but these steps are equal
and cancel in the growth rate $\alpha E - \mathrm{metab}$, so they leave the
growth curve smooth. Species with $t_0 \ge 0$ have $s = 0$, so
$\tilde A = A$, $\tilde B = B$ and nothing about their construction changes.

## Summary

The construction reproduces a von Bertalanffy growth curve in a mizer steady
state by assigning each term of the energy budget a definite role:

| Term | Choice | Purpose |
|------|--------|---------|
| Encounter $E(w)$ | $\tilde A(w)\,w^{n}/\alpha$, with $\tilde A = A + s$ below $w_{mat}$ | supplies the allometric income; carries the speed-up's $w^{n}$ term |
| Metabolic loss | $\tilde B(w)\,w$, with $\tilde B = B + s/u_{mat}$ below $w_{mat}$, minus the reproduction investment | linear respiration; carries the speed-up's $w$ term and funds reproduction |
| Maturity ogive $\psi$ | mizer default with $w_{\text{repro,max}} = w_\infty$ | splits surplus into soma and reproduction |
| Speed-up $\Delta(w)$ | $s\,w^{n}(1 - (w/w_{mat})^{1-n})$, $s$ from @eq-age-s | corrects a negative $t_0$ without a discontinuity |

The result is a model whose steady-state somatic growth equals the von
Bertalanffy rate above maturity, reaches maturity at the von Bertalanffy age even
when $t_0 < 0$, and has a growth rate that is continuous everywhere, including at
the maturity weight.
